text: Thank you very much for the opportunity to come and visit Dublin to present some of the work we've been doing in Leeds on complete mesocolic excision. I'm Nick West, I'm one of the clinical lecturers in pathology and gastrointestinal pathologist based in Leeds working in the laboratory of Phil Quirk where we're trying to improve outcomes for patients with colorectal cancer by assessing the optimal surgical methods and feeding this back to surgical teams. Phil's done a lot of work in the nineteen eighties and nineteen nineties on rectal cancer and the importance of total mesorectal excision and the surgical grading of total mesorectal excision specimens and now we're trying to translate these same principles into mesocolic dissection for colon cancer.

So the importance of TME is now well appreciated around the world. Bill Heald first published his experience of the importance of total mesorectal excision in the early nineteen eighties and we know that in countries where total mesorectal excision has been introduced as standard practice, for example here shown on the Stockholm data, we see an improvement in outcomes for patients in the order of around ten percent improvement in survival. Now at the same time Phil Quirk was rigorously assessing rectal cancer specimens and in nineteen eighty six he first published his seminal paper on the importance of the circumferential resection margin for rectal cancer showing that tumors within one millimetre of the circumferential margin predicted a higher rate of local disease recurrence.

The next contribution from Phil was the introduction of mesorectal grading and Phil described a three-tiered grading system. We can see here on the slide on the left hand side the mesorectal plane dissection where the specimen has a nice intact mesorectal fascia in the TME plane. In the middle we've got the intramesorectal specimen where we see significant defects into the mesorectal envelope which don't extend down to the muscle tube. And then on the right hand side we've got the muscularis propria plane where we see major disruptions into the mesorectal envelope which extend down to expose the muscle tube. And Phil was able to show in the CRO-VII trial, shown on the right-hand side of the slide, the difference in local recurrence rates between muscularis propria plane excisions and the more intact specimens, with twice as many patients experiencing a local recurrence with muscularis propria plane surgery compared to the other two grades. And we've now got plenty of evidence that mesorectal grading is a worthwhile thing for pathologists to do and to feed back to surgeons.

This is a meta-analysis from Stephen Borch and Iris Nagtagal on over four thousand patients showing that mesorectal grading does predict the rate of both local and distant disease recurrence. So how and why should we think about implementing these same principles into mesocolic dissection? Well the reason why is that we know there's variation in outcome for patients with colon cancer. Here we've got two survival curves. The top one shows the difference in survival for operable colon cancer for the nine hospitals across the Stockholm region of Sweden. And you can see between the best performing hospital at the top and the worst performing hospital at the bottom around a twenty five percent difference in survival for operative colon cancer. In the bottom survival curve this is UK data from the English NHS Trust generated by Dr Eva Morris in Leeds and again we can see the variation from the best performing trust to the worst performing trust for operable colon cancer in the order of twenty five percent

The video shows two colon cancer specimens. The first specimen is short in length with minimal mesentery attached to the muscle tube, and the cross-sectional slices reveal extensive tears extending down to the muscle tube. The second specimen, resected around the same time, displays a well-preserved mesocolon around the tumor, with cross-sectional slices showing the mesocolon extensively lined by peritoneum and no defects in the mesocolic envelope.

We know also from the literature that if we standardise our approach to colonic cancer dissection this will translate into an improved outcome for patients. So here we've got data from Warren Encker's group published in nineteen seventy nine showing that the five year survival for curative patients is in the order of eighty percent which is far in excess of other centres in this time period before the introduction of adjuvant chemotherapy. And they describe a very standardised approach to both colonic and rectal cancer surgery. A slightly later study published by Lesbocki and colleagues demonstrated that standardization of the approach for colon cancer at the time around nineteen eighty led to a fifteen percent improvement in survival for patients with colon cancer in their institution.

And finally, we've got the data from the Erlangen Group in Germany, who have a very standardized approach to colon cancer dissection, including operating meticulously in the mesocolic plane with a central vascular ligation. And they've reported their outcomes on over thirteen hundred consecutive colon cancers, describing very impressive outcome data. including five-year disease-free survival of eighty-nine percent in all cases and seventy percent in stage three disease which is at least fifteen to twenty percent higher than that that we routinely see across the UK and also very low rates of local recurrence less than four percent.

The video shows a cross-sectional slice of a colon cancer specimen from the Erlangen group, highlighting an early tumor that has not invaded beyond the muscle wall. Two lymph nodes are visible in the mesentery: one near the bowel wall appears normal, while the other, located further away, contains metastatic tumor cells. The specimen demonstrates intact resection with high vascular ligation, contrasting with lower tie specimens from the UK.

So we've devised a mesocolic grading system which mirrors the mesorectal grading system that Phil Quirk first described. And we have three grades starting with the muscularis propria plane where we see major defects into the mesocolon which extend down onto the muscle tube. We've then got the intramesocolic plane where we see significant defects which don't expose the muscle and then we have the intact mesocolic plane where the surgeons have resected in the mesocolic fascial plane with an intact peritoneal surface where appropriate. The microanatomy of the mesocolic fascia has been studied in detail, and we know that there are two fascial planes, the parietal and the visceral fascia, and these two planes come together during embryological development, and Werner Hohenberger describes the CME approach as undoing embryology, turning back the clock and separating the two fascial planes, leaving the visceral fascia attached to the colonic specimen.

And why would that translate into a better outcome for patients? Well, we know that within this mesocolonic or mesorectal fascial envelope, all of the blood vessels, the lymphatics and the lymph nodes through which cancer spread are contained within that envelope. So by keeping the fascia intact, we keep the cancer intact and all the potential roots of dissemination. Now we've undergone some recent refinements in the mesocolic grading to try and improve the inter-observer variability in mesocolic grading. So we now perform the grading on the whole specimen which should ideally be the fresh specimen. So in Leeds we try and encourage our surgeons to send fresh specimens immediately to the pathology department so that we can assess the mesocolon before it undergoes formalin fixation and the artefacts associated with that.

The video shows a cross-sectional slice of a right hemicolectomy specimen with minimal ascending colonic mesentery attached. The mesentery is intact and covered by peritoneum, indicating a mesocolic plane excision.

The video shows a right hemicolectomy specimen with a defect in the transverse mesocolon, illustrating the Japanese drainage area principle. The ileocolic mesentery, responsible for lymphatic drainage of cecal cancer, remains intact, and the defect is outside this drainage area. The specimen is classified as a mesocolic plane excision despite isolated disruptions of peritoneal windows, which are common in laparoscopic surgery. Additional tissue at the retroperitoneal aspect is visible, which can appear irregular and potentially confuse pathologists during grading.

The video shows a laparoscopic dissection around the central tie, highlighting disruptions that extend into the mesentery. Some defects are separations of vessels due to technical aspects of the laparoscopic procedure, which do not warrant downgrading the specimen. A peritoneal window within a sigmoid resection is visible, where the fused fascia and peritoneum layer is perforated, yet the specimen is still considered a mesocolic plane excision. Additionally, a defect around the high tie extends into the mesentery, exposing disrupted lymph nodes, leading to specimen downgrading.

there is a much lower incidence of muscularis propria plane surgery with only thirteen percent in the rectum and half of all specimens being intact. So this shows that there is a problem in the colon and relatively poorer planes of surgery in colonic cancer dissections compared to the rectum and this probably reflects all the work we've done on TME and TME training over the years through the Pelican TME training program. We also know that there's a distribution difference in the gradings according to the site of the primary tumour with the best profile occurring in the left colon where we see a similar distribution to the mesorectal plane grades with the majority of specimens resected in the intact mesocolic plane and only a small number of muscularis propria plane excisions. However, once we move into the right colon, we start to see poorer planes of surgery with an increased rate of muscularis propria plane excisions, rising to the highest rate in transverse colon cancer where we see a significant number of specimens showing major defects into the mesentery and exposing the muscle tube. And this could be one of the reasons why transverse colon cancer is associated with poorer outcomes compared to right and left-sided cancers. When we followed up the lead patients for five-year overall survival, we could see a nice separation of the survival curves according to the planes of surgery, with the mesocolic planes having a fifteen percent better survival compared to the muscular is appropriate plane group. When we take out the stage three cases and look at stage three disease in isolation as a subpopulation, we can see that the differences in survival become exaggerated, with twice as many patients alive at five years with mesocolic plane surgery compared to surgery in the muscularis propria plane. And this remains statistically significantly different on multivariate analysis with a hazard ratio of nought point four five.

We were able to independently verify the results of the LEADS study through the MRC CLASSIC trial where we had a hundred and sixty one gradable colon cancers and when we look at the distribution of the gradings we can see very similar to LEADS with twenty five percent of specimens resected in the muscularis propria plane and only twenty two percent of specimens being a mesocolic plane dissection. And when we followed up the classic trial patients and combined the mesocolic and intramesocolic plane groups versus muscularis propria plane, we can see a difference of around fifteen percent, which is similar to the difference we see in the LEAD study. And again, when you pull out the stage three cases, you can see twice as many patients alive at five years compared to surgery in the muscularis propria plane, which validates almost perfectly the earlier LEAD study where we see exactly the same association. One additional piece of information we gained from CLASSIC was the local recurrence data and surgeons often say that local recurrence for colon cancer is not an issue and very rarely occurs. Now we know from CLASSIC that surgery in the muscularis propria plane which happened in twenty five percent of cases is associated with a local recurrence rate in the region of twenty percent. So we can now say from Classic that surgery in the muscularis propria plane is associated with a significant rate of local disease recurrence and therefore we should be doing everything we possibly can to avoid surgery within this plane.

The video shows a specimen resected in Tokyo with intact mesocolic plane dissection and high vascular ligations. The specimen is compared to German specimens, highlighting differences in the length of colon resected. The Japanese technique involves operating ten centimeters beyond the tumor and transecting the colon, contrasting with the Erlangen principle of extending beyond the next vascular arcade.

Now the term complete mesocholic excision is appearing in a number of articles in the literature, some of which are very complementary towards this term and its principles and some of which are less complementary including this article published from here in Dublin by Des Winter and Hogan who described complete mesocholic excision and its novelty and the two authors said that twenty years ago when Bill Heald first introduced the term total mesorectal excision he was not inventing a new operation but had just proposed a new name for what was already accepted surgical practice and now history repeats itself for the colon. Now we agree with Hogan and Winter that complete mesocolic excision is not a new concept. Here you can see an article published in nineteen oh nine over a hundred years ago from a surgeon and an anatomist in Leeds where they described the lymphatic drainage of colonic cancer and the principle of resecting the mesocolon intact with a high vascular tie to adequately clear the lymphatic drainage. So it's certainly not a new concept, but what we would take issue with is that this is not routine practice. From our experience by examining specimens around the UK and wider around the world, we do not yet see standardised mesocolic plane surgery with a high ligation for all patients. There is certainly some work to do in standardising colonic cancer surgery.

So what are the variables? Just to recap the three variables we see in colon cancer specimens. First of all there's the mesocolic plane, how well the surgeons have stuck to the mesocolic fascial plane and we know that this impacts on survival with the evidence coming from Leeds and the independent validatory evidence from Classic showing that mesocolic grading is associated with overall survival and from Classic showing an association with local disease recurrence. For the central ligation, we know that centres implementing central ligation as routine, such as in Erlangen and Tokyo, they report the best outcomes in the literature to date for colon cancer. So there seems to be some degree of additional benefit to high ligation, although the relative benefits of the mesocolic plane and the high ligation is yet to be deduced. Now the length of colon resected also differs between Erlangen and between Japan, but both groups are reporting similarly impressive survival. So it appears that the length of colon resected probably doesn't have the same influence on survival that the mesocolic grading and the high vascular ligation do.

So what's the evidence for the implementation of CME? When I first gave this talk in Erlangen in two thousand and thirteen there were only around thirty studies in the literature with the term complete mesocolic excision on PubMed When I ran the same search two years later, earlier this year, we've now jumped to over eighty publications, so we're starting to see a lot of evidence from around the world demonstrating that complete mesocolic excision is feasible and associated with better outcomes. And recently we took part in a consensus conference organised by Carl Sondheim in Bergen, Norway, where we were able to describe the optimal principles for complete mesocolic excision surgery and when to do this and how to do it and how pathologists should assess the specimens. Now we've developed some tissue morphometrical techniques shown here at the top right for examining the amount of tissue taken out around colonic cancers and we do this using fresh specimen photographs by quantitating the amount of tissue taken out between the tumour and the high tie and the area of mesentery and the length of bowel resected and we can use this to compare different surgical techniques around the world.

The video shows a comparison between standard UK surgery and complete mesocolic excision with high ligation for right-sided colon cancer specimens. The complete mesocolic excision technique results in an additional five centimeters of tissue between the tumor and the high tie, a longer length of colon resected, and a greater overall area of mesentery. This approach is associated with a higher lymph node yield and more resections in the mesocolic plane.

And we see the same pattern for left-sided tumours again with an extra five centimetres of tissue resected between the tumour and the high tie, additional length of colon, additional area of mesentery, more lymph nodes and more resections in the mesocolic plane. We've been able to use the same techniques to compare the German and the Japanese approach, and we can see here for right-sided tumours there's a very minimal difference between the tumour and the high tie, in the order of one point five centimetres for right-sided excisions. We can see the majority of the difference is in the length of the colon resectors, so the Japanese specimens are around nine centimetres shorter on average, with less area of mesentery and less lymph node yield, but a similarly impressive mesocolic plane resection rate. When we look at the left-sided specimens, no difference in the measurement between the tumour and the high-tie, but quite a significant difference in the length of colon resected, with an additional twenty-six centimetres of colon resected in the Erlangen specimens compared to the Japanese. And again, this is associated with more mesentery and more lymph nodes in the Erlangen specimens compared to Japan, but similarly impressive rates of mesocolic plane surgery. We've been able to now look at this in more detail in the stage three group with a follow-up paper where we've also been able to compare both Erlangen and Japan to the standard surgery in the UK, where we've taken Leeds as the example, showing that both the German and the Japanese specimens do take out more tissue between the tumour and the high tie, despite the differences between the two techniques in terms of length of colon resected. We've also worked with Robin Kennedy and Ian Jenkins at St Mark's in London looking at the value of laparoscopic complete mesocolic excision and compared this to the open approach in Erlangen and we've been able to show that in St Mark's using laparoscopic surgery the specimens are very very similar to open surgery in Erlangen with only a very small difference between the tumour and high time measurement for right-sided specimens

and no major differences in the area of mesentery resected and certainly no difference in the mesocolic plane rate. And for left-sided specimens, no difference in the tumour to high time measurements and similarly impressive mesocolic plane dissection rates. So we now know that laparoscopic CME can be effectively applied in the same way that open CME can. Finally, we've done some work in Denmark looking at the effect of multidisciplinary education on colonic cancer surgery and we were able to assess one of the hospitals, Hillerod Hospital, who had been to Erlangen to undergo colonic cancer training from Professor Hohenberger and implement this as standard practice for colon cancer in their unit compared to the other five hospitals in the Copenhagen region. We applied the same principles as the other studies by taking photographs of the fresh specimens and we were able to show that the Hilarod group, after switching their approach to CME, now resected more tissue between the tumour and the high tie, a greater length of colon, a greater area of mesentery, more lymph nodes and more likely to be resected in the mesocolic plane compared to the other five hospitals. And we saw the same effect both on right and left-sided resections.

We've gone on to do some further work with Soren Lauberg in the Aarhus region of Denmark to show a similar effect that multidisciplinary education and surgical training can improve the quality of mesocolic plane excisions. So how do outcomes change following these multidisciplinary educational initiatives? We've now got early data from the Hillerod group that actually switching to a higher rate of mesocolic plane surgery is associated with improved survival. This data was published in Lancet Oncology in January of this year. and we can see that the Hilarod specimens were associated with around a ten percent better survival compared to non-CME specimens from the other hospitals across the region. Since this data was collected the Danish governments have funded a national training program in colonic cancer and all of the hospitals within the Copenhagen region of Denmark have undergone surgical training and you can see on the pie charts there is now a difference in mesocolic grading from before the regional education program across the whole region where we've seen the number of mesocolic plane excisions improve and the number of intramesocolic plane excisions reduce, and this should therefore translate into an improvement in outcome for patients.

We've got some ongoing studies across the world, one of which is a UK study, the FOXTROC trial, where we're looking at the value of preoperative chemotherapy for advanced colon cancer, and this may yield additional survival benefits in addition to standardising the mesocolic grading. We're also doing some educational initiatives within the UK, starting with a pilot programme in the Yorkshire region funded by Yorkshire Cancer Research, where we've brought all of the local surgeons and pathologists together to study the evidence and to try and improve the number of mesocolic plane excisions across the region. We're also involved in the T-REX study which is being run through Professor Sugihara in Japan where we're analyzing the differences between the European approach of taking an extended length of colon versus the Japanese approach of taking a very short length of colon to understand the differences in lymph node yields and the distribution of positive lymph nodes in order to understand which one of these two techniques is optimal. And finally, we're undertaking the GLSEN trial, which is a collaboration between Leeds and the Mater Hospital here in Dublin, looking at the value of intraoperative fluorescence to identify positive lymph nodes and therefore stratify the operative approach, with the concept being that positive disease in the mesentery may undergo a high tie

So just to summarize I've shown you that there is significant variation in the quality of colon cancer specimens and there is significant variation in colon cancer outcomes and I think there is good evidence that the two are linked. We've shown that the plane of surgery is important and the evidence for this comes from the lead study and validated by the classic trial with mesocolic plane surgery having a better outcome and this is exaggerated in stage three disease. We know that the addition of complete musicolic excision with central vascular ligation appears to be associated with additional benefits and the evidence for this comes from the impressive survivals reported in Erlangen and in Japan when compared to the rest of the world. There's evidence from Denmark that multidisciplinary education is beneficial. It certainly improves the quality of specimens and we've demonstrated that in Hillerod and then wider across the region. We've also now seen the longer-term survival improvements in Hillerod compared to the other hospitals. We know that laparoscopic surgery can effectively produce the same high quality specimen to open surgery and we've demonstrated this through the work we've done with Robin and Ian at St Mark's.

And finally, pathological quality control is the process that underpins all of these developments and drives forward the improvement in surgery and therefore improvements in outcomes for patients. So we need to encourage greater collaboration between surgeons and pathologists with routine pathological feedback by taking photographs of specimens after resection and the demonstration of any defects at the multidisciplinary team meetings. So we now routinely photograph all of our specimens as they come into the department, preferably fresh, although formerly in fixed specimen photographs are perfectly acceptable, with additional images taken of the cross-sectional slices, and we will try and show these in the multidisciplinary team meetings to discuss any issues with the mesocolic gradings. So I just want to finish with this quote from the eminent UK pathologist Cuthbert Dukes who recognised the importance of high quality surgery very early on during his practice and Dukes said, I should not choose the operation but I should choose the surgeon to do it and I should choose him with great care. and I think that makes complete sense. The surgeon is very important in the cure of patients with colonic cancer and if they do a good job and resect the specimen in the mesocolic plane then the patient is more likely to survive and we've had the pleasure of working with a number of surgeons from the UK, across Europe and beyond over the years who've enabled us to do our work on mesocolic grading and tissue morphometry to identify the optimal techniques which are associated with the best outcomes for patients.

But I think the one thing that Dukes forgot to mention is that one should also choose the right pathologist because if we don't identify any defects in the specimen and feed this back to the team to aid their learning, if we don't identify all of the lymph nodes and all of the extramural vascular invasion, the peritoneal disease and the margin positivity from which patients may benefit from adjuvant therapy, then we're doing our patients a disservice. So we need further collaborations between pathology and surgeons to improve outcomes for our patients. So I'd just like to thank all of our local, our national and our international collaborators who contributed to the data presented. And at the bottom, I need to acknowledge our funders, Yorkshire Cancer Research, the National Institute for Health Research, the Pathological Society and the Academy of Medical Sciences for funding the work we do in Leeds.
